Fitting N-mixture models

Analysing Ecological Data with Detection Error

Author

Péter Sólymos

Published

April 19, 2026

Preamble

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(unmarked)
    library(detect)
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    theme_set(theme_light())
})
set.seed(1234)

Simulating count data

Simulating counts is similar to occupancy. We need a count distribution. The most basic count distribution is Poisson which has 1 parameter, \lambda, which is the mean. The variance also happens to equal the mean. Use the rpois() function in R to generate random numbers from the poisson distribution. Here, N_i (i=1,\ldots,n) is the abundance (number of individuals) at the ith location.

n <- 50
n2 <- 500
p.true <- 0.6

lambda.true <- 4.2

N <- rpois(n = n, lambda = lambda.true)

table(N)
N
 0  1  2  3  4  5  6  7 10 
 1  2  5 15  8  9  7  2  1 
summary(N)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
      0       3       4       4       5      10 
table(N) |> plot(ylab = "Frequency")

Observation error for counts

Here is how we introduce observation error to count data:

  • select a location,
  • take the count N_i,
  • for each individual (1, 2, \ldots, N_i), use the Bernoulli distribution with probability p to determine if the individual was detected (1) or not (0)

The number of individuals detected (Y_i) is less than or equal to N_i: Y_i \le N_i. For example, if N_i = 4, Y_i can be 0, 1, 2, 3, or 4.

To express things mon concisely, we can also use a Binomial distribution with N_i as the number of trials and probability p:

Y <- rbinom(n = n, size = N, prob = p.true)

print(table(N = N, Y = Y), zero.print = ".")
    Y
N    0 1 2 3 4 5 7
  0  1 . . . . . .
  1  . 2 . . . . .
  2  2 2 1 . . . .
  3  . 4 5 6 . . .
  4  . 2 2 3 1 . .
  5  . . 3 3 3 . .
  6  . . . . 2 5 .
  7  . . . . . 2 .
  10 . . . . . . 1

Count data with multiple visits

When visiting each site T times, the observed data will be organized in a n by T matrix as how we saw it for occupancy:

Y <- matrix(NA, n, T)
for (t in 1:T) {
    Y[, t] <- rbinom(n = n, size = N, prob = p.true)
}

# alternatively
# Y <- replicate(T, rbinom(n = n, size = N, prob = p.true))

Multiple-visit count model have similar assumptions as occupancy models, most importantly that N_{it}=N_i. This condition is also referred to as the closed population assumption, i.e. there is no immigration, emigration, birth, or death between visits.

data.frame(N = N, Visit = Y) |> head()
  N Visit
1 2     1
2 5     5
3 5     3
4 5     4
5 6     5
6 5     4

As we saw before, we can use the Y_max to fit the naive count model, but instead of the plogis() function, we use log() as the inverse of the logarithmic link used for Poisson GLM:

Y_max <- apply(Y, 1, max)
m4 <- glm(Y_max ~ 1, family = poisson)
coef(m4)
(Intercept) 
  0.8329091 
exp(coef(m4))
(Intercept) 
        2.3

With enough visits, tha Y_max will approach the latent variable N:

N2 <- rpois(n = n2, lambda = lambda.true)
Tvals <- c(1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50)

data.frame(
    T = Tvals,
    Y_max = sapply(Tvals, \(T) {
        mean(apply(
            replicate(T, rbinom(n = n, size = N, prob = p.true)),
            1, max
        ))
    })
) |> ggplot(aes(x = T, y = Y_max)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = lambda.true, lty = 2, col = 2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = mean(N), lty = 2, col = 4) +
    geom_line() +
    ylim(0, NA) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = Tvals) +
    theme_light()

Fitting N-mixture models

Fit the multiple-visit count model, the so called N-mixture model, using the pcount() function of the unmarked R package.

The unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount() function is used to organize the count data:

umf <- unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount(y = Y)
summary(umf)
unmarkedFrame Object

50 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 1 
Mean number of observations per site: 1 
Sites with at least one detection: 43 

Tabulation of y observations:
 0  1  2  3  4  5  6 
 7  8 16  7  7  4  1 
m5 <- unmarked::pcount(~1 ~ 1, umf, K = 50)
m5

Call:
unmarked::pcount(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf, K = 50)
Warning in sqrt(diag(vcov(obj, fixedOnly = fixedOnly))): NaNs produced
Abundance (log-scale):
 Estimate  SE   z P(>|z|)
     2.12 NaN NaN     NaN
Warning in sqrt(diag(vcov(obj, fixedOnly = fixedOnly))): NaNs produced
Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate  SE   z P(>|z|)
   -0.964 NaN NaN     NaN

AIC: 185.6474 
Number of sites: 50
Warning in sqrt(diag(v)): NaNs produced
Warning: Large or missing SE values. Be very cautious using these results.
plogis(coef(m5, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.2760193 
exp(coef(m5, type = "state"))
lam(Int) 
8.332899
CautionReferences

Royle, 2004. N-mixture models for estimating population size from spatially replicated counts. Biometrics, 60:108–115. Fulltext, DOI 10.1111/j.0006-341X.2004.00142.x

The K argument of the pcount() function is the upper index of integration for N-mixture. This will make more sense once we take a look at the likelihood function in the next section.

N-mixture likelihood

The likelihood function for the N-mixture model can be written as:

L(p, \lambda; y_{1,1}, \ldots, y_{n,T}) = \prod^{n}_{i=1} \sum^{K}_{N_{i}=Y_{max,i}} \prod^{T}_{t=1} e^{-\lambda_{i}}\frac{\lambda_{i}^{N_{i}}}{N_{i}!}\left(\begin{array}{c} N_{i}\\ Y_{it} \end{array}\right) p^{Y_{it}} (1-p)^{N_{i}-Y_{it}}

This distribution is a mixture of Poisson and Binomial distributions.

The K value strictly speaking should be \infty, but the estimates won’t change much as long as K is large enough relative to the maximum of N_i. Of course for real data we do not know what the values of N_i are. The numerical integration (summation) for site i goes from Y_{max,i} (Y_max) to K, because we know that Y_{it} \le N_i.

We can write this as a log likelihood function:

logL_fun_pcount_R <- function(Y, p, lambda, n, T, Y_max, K) {
    L <- rep(NA, n)
    for (i in 1:n) {
        S <- 0
        for (Nit in Y_max[i]:K) {
            v <- 0
            for (j in 1:T) {
                v <- v + dbinom(Y[i, j], Nit, p, log = TRUE) +
                    dpois(Nit, lambda, log = TRUE)
            }
            S <- S + exp(v)
        }
        L[i] <- S
    }
    sum(log(L))
}

# using C code from unmarked - much faster
logL_fun_pcount_C <- function(Y, p, lambda, n, T, Y_max, K = 50) {
    nll <- unmarked:::nll_pcount(
        beta = c(log(lambda), qlogis(p)),
        n_param = c(1, 1, 0),
        y = Y,
        X = matrix(1, n, 1),
        V = matrix(1, n * T, 1),
        X_offset = rep(0, n),
        V_offset = matrix(1, n * T, 1),
        K = K,
        Kmin = Y_max,
        mixture = 1,
        threads = 1
    )
    -nll
}
g <- 50
grid <- expand.grid(
    p = seq(0, 1, length.out = g),
    lambda = seq(0, lambda.true * 2, length.out = g),
    logL = NA
)

logL_fun_pcount <- logL_fun_pcount_C
for (i in 1:nrow(grid)) {
    grid$logL[i] <- logL_fun_pcount(
        Y = Y,
        p = grid$p[i],
        lambda = grid$lambda[i],
        n = n, T = T, Y_max = Y_max, K = 50
    )
}
grid$logL[is.infinite(grid$logL)] <- NA

The images showing the likelihood surface:

image(
    list(
        x = unique(grid$p),
        y = unique(grid$lambda),
        z = matrix(exp(grid$logL), sqrt(nrow(grid)))
    ),
    xlab = "p",
    ylab = expression(lambda)
)
abline(h = lambda.true, v = p.true, col = 1, lwd = 1)
grid |> ggplot(aes(x = p, y = lambda, z = exp(logL))) +
    geom_contour_filled(show.legend = FALSE) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = lambda.true) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = p.true) +
    xlab("p") +
    ylab(expression(lambda)) +
    theme_light()
grid[which.max(grid$logL), ]
             p   lambda      logL
1022 0.4285714 3.428571 -90.82372
Note

We will circle back to these plots later, feel free to explore it with different settings of n, T, p, and \lambda.

Field data analysis

We will use the ABMI data, which has 3 equal (3.3 min) time intervals for each point count visit. We use the point level data, but only for the center point, so we can keep sample size small and run the code quickly. When analyzing such data sets for real, we would use all the data and not subset it like this.

if (!interactive()) {
    d <- readRDS("../data/ab_birds_site_year_pt.rds")
} else {
    d <- readRDS("data/ab_birds_site_year_pt.rds")
}

spp <- "yellowrumped_warbler"

x <- data.frame(
    y1 = ifelse(d$visits$visit1[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    y2 = ifelse(d$visits$visit2[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    y3 = ifelse(d$visits$visit3[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    n1 = d$visits$visit1[, spp],
    n2 = d$visits$visit2[, spp],
    n3 = d$visits$visit3[, spp],
    d$vars
)
x$yday <- as.POSIXlt(x$fielddate)$yday / 365
x$hr <- x$fieldtime / 24
x$y_max <- pmax(x$y1, x$y2, x$y3)
x$n_max <- pmax(x$n1, x$n2, x$n3)
x <- x |>
    filter(
        hr > 0.1,
        hr < 0.6,
        yday > 0.37,
        yday < 0.5
    )
x0 <- x[x$point == 1, ]

Naive abundance estimates for the 3 visits:

m_v1 <- glm(n1 ~ 1, data = x0, family = poisson)
coef(m_v1)
(Intercept) 
 -0.9800302 
exp(coef(m_v1))
(Intercept) 
  0.3752998 
mean(x0$n1)
[1] 0.3752998
m_v2 <- glm(n2 ~ 1, data = x0, family = poisson)
coef(m_v2)
(Intercept) 
 -0.9928921 
exp(coef(m_v2))
(Intercept) 
  0.3705036 
mean(x0$n2)
[1] 0.3705036
m_v3 <- glm(n3 ~ 1, data = x0, family = poisson)
coef(m_v3)
(Intercept) 
   -1.02914 
exp(coef(m_v3))
(Intercept) 
  0.3573141 
mean(x0$n3)
[1] 0.3573141

Use the maximum count to fit the naive model:

m_max <- glm(n_max ~ 1, data = x0, family = poisson)
coef(m_max)
(Intercept) 
 -0.6859787 
exp(coef(m_max))
(Intercept) 
  0.5035971 
mean(x0$n_max)
[1] 0.5035971

N-mixture model using the pcount() function:

# here N-mix version using pcount
umf <- unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount(y = x0[, c("n1", "n2", "n3")])
summary(umf)
unmarkedFrame Object

834 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 3 
Mean number of observations per site: 3 
Sites with at least one detection: 264 

Tabulation of y observations:
   0    1    2    3    4 
1884  387  170   51   10 
m_mv <- unmarked::pcount(~1 ~ 1, umf, K = 100)
m_mv

Call:
unmarked::pcount(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf, K = 100)

Abundance (log-scale):
 Estimate     SE     z  P(>|z|)
   -0.634 0.0494 -12.8 1.19e-37

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE  z  P(>|z|)
    0.814 0.074 11 3.73e-28

AIC: 2898.027 
Number of sites: 834
plogis(coef(m_mv, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.6929904 
exp(coef(m_mv, type = "state"))
 lam(Int) 
0.5305777

Use covariates to model abundance and detection:

umf2 <- unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount(
    y = x0[, c("n1", "n2", "n3")],
    siteCovs = x0[, c("pconif", "thf")],
    obsCovs = list(
        hr = x0[, c("hr", "hr", "hr")],
        yday = x0[, c("yday", "yday", "yday")]
    )
)
summary(umf2)
unmarkedFrame Object

834 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 3 
Mean number of observations per site: 3 
Sites with at least one detection: 264 

Tabulation of y observations:
   0    1    2    3    4 
1884  387  170   51   10 

Site-level covariates:
     pconif            thf         
 Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.00000  
 1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.00000  
 Median :0.0000   Median :0.06333  
 Mean   :0.2740   Mean   :0.34896  
 3rd Qu.:0.6036   3rd Qu.:0.86650  
 Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.00000  

Observation-level covariates:
       hr              yday       
 Min.   :0.1493   Min.   :0.3890  
 1st Qu.:0.2111   1st Qu.:0.4082  
 Median :0.2257   Median :0.4192  
 Mean   :0.2516   Mean   :0.4273  
 3rd Qu.:0.2562   3rd Qu.:0.4493  
 Max.   :0.5271   Max.   :0.4822  
m_mv2 <- unmarked::pcount(
    ~ hr + I(hr^2) + yday ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + thf,
    umf2,
    K = 100
)
m_mv2

Call:
unmarked::pcount(formula = ~hr + I(hr^2) + yday ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + 
    thf, data = umf2, K = 100)

Abundance (log-scale):
            Estimate    SE     z  P(>|z|)
(Intercept)   -0.981 0.112 -8.78 1.57e-18
pconif         3.568 0.537  6.65 3.00e-11
I(pconif^2)   -2.361 0.515 -4.58 4.63e-06
thf           -1.632 0.234 -6.98 2.92e-12

Detection (logit-scale):
            Estimate    SE       z P(>|z|)
(Intercept)    3.700  2.01  1.8442  0.0652
hr             0.439  9.56  0.0459  0.9634
I(hr^2)        0.751 15.13  0.0496  0.9604
yday          -7.428  3.69 -2.0151  0.0439

AIC: 2578.76 
Number of sites: 834
Warning: Large or missing SE values. Be very cautious using these results.
m_mv@AIC
[1] 2898.027
m_mv2@AIC
[1] 2578.76
summary(fitted(m_mv2))
       V1                V2                V3         
 Min.   :0.04181   Min.   :0.04181   Min.   :0.04181  
 1st Qu.:0.06299   1st Qu.:0.06299   1st Qu.:0.06299  
 Median :0.23807   Median :0.23807   Median :0.23807  
 Mean   :0.36495   Mean   :0.36495   Mean   :0.36495  
 3rd Qu.:0.71320   3rd Qu.:0.71320   3rd Qu.:0.71320  
 Max.   :1.04481   Max.   :1.04481   Max.   :1.04481  
colMeans(umf2@y)
       n1        n2        n3 
0.3752998 0.3705036 0.3573141 
X <- model.matrix(m_mv2@formula[-2], x0)
fit_mv2 <- exp(X %*% coef(m_mv2, type = "state"))
hist(fit_mv2)
summary(fit_mv2)
       V1         
 Min.   :0.07331  
 1st Qu.:0.10041  
 Median :0.36936  
 Mean   :0.54835  
 3rd Qu.:1.07995  
 Max.   :1.44325  
data.frame(x0, lam = fit_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = pconif, y = lam, col = thf)) +
    geom_point() +
    ylim(0, NA)
data.frame(x0, lam = fit_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(n_max), y = lam)) +
    geom_boxplot() +
    ylim(0, NA)
m_max2 <- glm(n_max ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + thf, data = x0, family = poisson)

data.frame(lam = fit_mv2, n_max = fitted(m_max2)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = n_max, y = lam)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_abline(lty = 2) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
mean(fit_mv2 / fitted(m_max2))
[1] 1.070923
Z <- model.matrix(m_mv2@formula[-3], x0)
head(Z)
            (Intercept)        hr    I(hr^2)      yday
1_2009_1              1 0.2354167 0.05542101 0.4410959
1001_2011_1           1 0.2437500 0.05941406 0.4465753
1002_2011_1           1 0.2465278 0.06077595 0.4356164
1003_2011_1           1 0.3888889 0.15123457 0.4383562
1007_2008_1           1 0.2666667 0.07111111 0.4054795
1008_2008_1           1 0.2020833 0.04083767 0.4164384
det_mv2 <- plogis(Z %*% coef(m_mv2, type = "det"))
summary(det_mv2)
       V1        
 Min.   :0.5642  
 1st Qu.:0.6273  
 Median :0.6762  
 Mean   :0.6643  
 3rd Qu.:0.6975  
 Max.   :0.7512  
hist(det_mv2)
data.frame(x0, p_det = det_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = yday, y = p_det, col = hr)) +
    geom_point() +
    ylim(0, 1)

What do we get when only using 2 visits?

umf_v2 <- unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount(y = x0[, c("n1", "n2")])
m_mv_v2 <- unmarked::pcount(~1 ~ 1, umf_v2, K = 100)
m_mv_v2

Call:
unmarked::pcount(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf_v2, K = 100)

Abundance (log-scale):
 Estimate    SE     z  P(>|z|)
   -0.712 0.053 -13.4 3.54e-41

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE    z  P(>|z|)
     1.15 0.114 10.1 4.82e-24

AIC: 2172.117 
Number of sites: 834
plogis(coef(m_mv_v2, type = "det"))
  p(Int) 
0.759897 
exp(coef(m_mv_v2, type = "state"))
 lam(Int) 
0.4907264

How about only using 1 visit?

umf_v1 <- unmarked::unmarkedFramePCount(y = x0[, c("n1"), drop = FALSE])
m_mv_v1 <- unmarked::pcount(~1 ~ 1, umf_v1, K = 100)
m_mv_v1

Call:
unmarked::pcount(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf_v1, K = 100)

Abundance (log-scale):
 Estimate  SE        z P(>|z|)
   -0.366 336 -0.00109   0.999

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate  SE        z P(>|z|)
    0.166 733 0.000226       1

AIC: 1405.514 
Number of sites: 834
Warning: Large or missing SE values. Be very cautious using these results.
plogis(coef(m_mv_v1, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.5413588 
exp(coef(m_mv_v1, type = "state"))
 lam(Int) 
0.6932552

Single visit estimates for the constant abundance model are not reliable.

There are tools out there (e.g. data cloning) that you can used to see if the model you are fitting is identifiable or not.

Exercise: Try "P", "NB", "ZIP" mixtures for pcount().
